prompt:
A TrailForge Itinerary Management System that includes itinerary planning, governed data management, secure collaboration, and controlled media handling for travel operations teams that must run fully offline. Users sign in with a local username and password and land in a Vue.js workspace where they can create itineraries, add attractions from an internal catalog, and reorder stops using drag-and-drop with immediate visual feedback and autosave indicators. The planner view calculates estimated travel distance in miles and time in minutes using locally stored attraction coordinates and configurable average speeds (default 25 mph urban, 55 mph highway), then warns on schedule conflicts when two items overlap by 15 minutes or more or when the daily plan exceeds 12 hours of activities. Users can export or import itineraries as CSV/Excel and as an offline "sync package" file for cross-device transfer; each import produces a readable receipt showing accepted rows, rejected rows, and correction hints. A built-in resource center lets users upload images and documents for attractions and itineraries with thumbnail previews, client-side progress display, and server-confirmed validations, enforcing a 20 MB limit per file, allowing only PDF/DOCX/XLSX/CSV and JPG/PNG, and rejecting mismatched extensions versus detected MIME type. The message center provides unified notification drafting with reusable templates and variable rendering (such as traveler name and departure time), showing a timeline of in-app messages and reserving connectors for SMS/email/push without requiring them for offline operation; frequency capping limits outreach to 3 messages per user per day and 1 per hour per template category.

The backend uses FastAPI to expose REST-style endpoints that the Vue.js frontend calls over local-network HTTPS, issuing session cookies for browser usage and revocable API tokens for device-to-device sync and internal integrations, with tokens expiring after 30 days by default. PostgreSQL stores users, organizations, roles, permissions, itineraries, itinerary versions, attractions, uploaded assets, message templates, delivery attempts, audit logs, and data lineage. RBAC is enforced at resource granularity so an Organization Admin can manage datasets/projects, a Planner can edit assigned itineraries, and an Auditor can view history and logs without modifying content; sensitive actions like permission changes and bulk merges require re-entering the password within the last 10 minutes. Data quality rules are enforced on write and on batch import, including required fields, numeric ranges (for example, attraction duration 5–720 minutes), uniqueness constraints, and referential integrity; duplicate detection uses deterministic keys (normalized name plus city/state) and provides a merge workflow that preserves change history. Security includes TLS in transit, encryption at rest for stored credentials and API tokens, masking of sensitive fields in logs, immutable audit trails for 1 year, configurable data retention (default 3 years for itineraries), and nightly encrypted backups to a designated local folder with a 14-day rotation and a documented restore procedure. File assets are stored on local disk through an object-storage abstraction that can later target CDN/object storage, while offline-safe checksums, basic signature validation, and a quarantine flag support future scanning workflows; unreferenced files are automatically cleaned up after 30 days. stack:Frontend stack

modern_frontend_framework

Backend stack

Python

Database

PostgreSQL
